subject: mathematics

core_competencies.justification: Section 5.1 is wholly dedicated to deepening understanding of mean and median: the balancing-point model, reasoning about how adding/removing values and uniformly shifting or scaling all values affects the mean (with algebraic proofs), computing mean and median from frequency tables (WE-3), and recovering an unknown value from a given mean (WE-1, WE-2). This is the primary organisational purpose of the first half of the chapter.

Section 5.2 is wholly dedicated to selecting and interpreting graphical representations of data: line graphs are introduced as the appropriate choice for time-series data (with worked examples on temperature, space launches, and rainfall), a choropleth infographic is read and interpreted, activity strips are decoded and created, and a smooth sleep-duration curve is analysed. This constitutes a fully independent strand from C-5.1.

adjunct_competencies.justification: WE-1 and WE-2 (Finding the Unknown) explicitly pose and solve linear equations — 349 + w = 392 and z/15 = 25.6 — to recover missing values from a known mean. E-7, E-8, and E-9 require the same equation-forming and solving skill in the exercise set.

The algebraic proofs in section 5.1 — that shifting all values by a constant shifts the mean by the same constant, and that scaling all values scales the mean — use deductive argument with symbolic algebra. E-6 and E-19 require students to evaluate statements about mean and median as always/sometimes/never true with justification, applying inductive and deductive logic.

The spreadsheets sub-section in section 5.1 introduces cell references, range notation (e.g., B3:G3, D2:D6), and formula syntax (=SUM(), =AVERAGE()), requiring students to think in terms of symbolic representation and algorithmic operations — foundational computational-thinking skills applied to a real data-organisation task.

dissolution_test: This chapter builds the student's ability to reason about and interpret data by deepening the balance-point model of mean and median, proving how uniform transformations affect them, and applying multiple graphical representations — line graphs, infographics, and activity strips — to draw valid inferences from real-world data.
